FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2-4 follow-up data, N = 2,749, baseline age 11.9 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal mediation analysis with path coefficients examining relationship between gaming/caffeine use and sleep

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper analyzes VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, generic video gaming scale) paired with sleep outcomes. VGAQ is generic gaming without explicit phone/mobile modality. Step 2: Not a composite. Per binding clarification #2, gaming alone without explicit mobile/phone modality fails IC2. No qualifying phone/SM-specific variable paired with individual-trait outcome.

notes: Gaming-only analysis without isolation of mobile/phone-based gaming. VGAQ is a generic video gaming addiction measure.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2-4 data, approximately 7,260 participants, KSADS-COMP ADHD module and VGAQ administered

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models and parallel mediation model examining longitudinal association between ADHD diagnosis and VGAQ scores with nine psychosocial mediators

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, Frontiers in Psychiatry, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper analyzes VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), a generic video gaming addiction scale without explicit phone/mobile modality. Per binding clarification #2, gaming alone fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with separately-measured phone/SM variable. No phone/SM variable with separate coefficient exists in this paper. VGAQ is the gaming variable, not a phone/SM variable.

notes: Analysis centers on VGAQ (generic video gaming addiction scale) as outcome, not a qualifying phone/SM variable. No isolation of mobile/phone-based gaming.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, participants aged 9-10 at baseline, N = 9,474, longitudinal data 2016-2022

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) for screen time and social media patterns; multivariable linear regression examining associations with anxiety/depression

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, BMC Public Health, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper reports separate social media trajectory analyses (5 groups) paired with anxiety/depression outcomes (Table 4, Figure 1b). Social media qualifies as phone/SM-specific variable. Results show significant associations (e.g., Group 3 "Increasing-then-Decreasing Use" β = 1.44, 95% CI [0.75, 2.12] for anxiety; p < 0.001) with anxiety/depression (individual-trait-level mental health outcomes). Other-side requirement met.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported for social media ↔ anxiety/depression relationship: linear regression coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values (Table 4). Main screen time groups also show significant associations with anxiety/depression (β ranging 0.55 to 1.44 for anxiety; p < 0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis: multivariable linear models with complex survey weights, group-based trajectory modeling, interaction terms examined. Numeric quantification throughout with p-values, CIs, and effect estimates reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline to Year 3 follow-up). Screen time and social media analyzed as DV in all analyses (no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in paper). No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis present. All three conditions for FT-EC7 exclusion met.
notes: Paper meets IC1-IC4 but is excluded by FT-EC7 longitudinal SM-DV-only rule. Social media trajectory groups (DV) paired with anxiety/depression (DV covariate structure), with alcohol/e-cigarette explored as moderator (not primary predictor structure). All analyses treat SM as outcome, not predictor.
